column5: Description

column2: Migration begins after last ice age
column5: Migration from the Middle East to Europe begins after the last ice age. (Status: needs confirmation)
Ancient Celts arrive in the British Isles and Western Europe from the Steppes; white skin genetics arrive in Europe with Middle Easterners and Central Asians. (Status: needs confirmation)
Research has found that Mikhaylovka, in the lower Dnieper river, Ukraine, formed the Core Yamnaya culture during this period.

column4: Region between Southern Bug, Dniester, and Ural rivers (Pontic-Caspian steppe)
column5: The Yamnaya culture, also known as the Pit Grave culture or Ochre Grave culture, was a late Copper Age to early Bronze Age archaeological culture of the region between the Southern Bug, Dniester, and Ural rivers (the Pontic–Caspian steppe). It was discovered by Vasily Gorodtsov following his archaeological excavations near the Donets River in 1901–1903. Its name derives from its characteristic burial tradition: Yamnaya is a Russian adjective meaning 'related to pits (yama)', as these people used to bury their dead in tumuli (kurgans) containing simple pit chambers.
The Hurrians were in the region as of the late 3rd millennium BCE. A king of Urkesh with a Hurrian name, Tupkish, was found on a clay sealing dated approximately 2300 BCE at Tell Mozan.
The first recorded inscription of the Hurrian language was of Tish-atal (approximately 21st century BCE), king of Urkesh.
In Biblical record, this is the time Jacob and his family migrated to Egypt due to famine in Canaan.
Canaanites, including future Hyksos, migrated to Egypt due to famine. (Approximate date)
Gradual influx of Semitic peoples into Egypt's eastern Delta region.

Later on, Hurrians made up the main population of Mitanni, which was firstly known as Ḫabigalbat, at Babylonia, in two texts of the late Old Babylonian period, during the reign of Ammi-Saduqa, in low middle chronology.

The Hyksos, often linked to the Scythians, controlled northern Egypt before being expelled and migrating northward. The Hyksos are theorized to have carried Mesopotamian religious symbols (e.g., menorahs, winged disks) into Central Asia, potentially influencing later Scythian and Saka cultures. This migration could have laid the groundwork for the spread of Israelite-like symbols and practices across Eurasia.

Mitanni, also known as Hanigalbat or Hani-Rabbat in Assyrian records, or Naharin in Egyptian texts, was a Hurrian-speaking state in northern Syria and southeast Anatolia (modern-day Turkey) with Indo-Aryan linguistic and political influences. Since no histories, royal annals or chronicles have yet been found in its excavated sites, knowledge about Mitanni is sparse compared to the other powers in the area, and dependent on what its neighbors commented in their texts.

column4: Ranged from Kizzuwatna (west) to Arraphe (east), Tunip (south) to Lake Van (north)
column5: The Mitanni Empire was a strong regional power limited by the Hittites to the north, Egyptians to the west, Kassites to the south, and later by the Assyrians to the east. At its maximum extent Mitanni ranged as far west as Kizzuwatna by the Taurus Mountains, Tunip in the south, Arraphe in the east, and north to Lake Van. Their sphere of influence is shown in Hurrian place names, personal names and the spread through Syria and the Levant of a distinct pottery type, Nuzi ware.
Prince Moses led the Exodus of Hebrews who recognized Abraham. (Approximate date)
Expulsion of remaining Hyksos from Egypt to Canaan.
Period of enslavement for Israelites remaining in Egypt, according to some interpretations.
Description
Early Scythian culture emerges in the Pontic-Caspian (Crimean) steppe.

The Tauri (/ˈtaʊəri/; Ταῦροι in Ancient Greek), or Taurians, also Scythotauri, Tauri Scythae, Tauroscythae (Pliny, H. N. 4.85) were an ancient people settled on the southern coast of the Crimea peninsula, inhabiting the Crimean Mountains in the 1st millennium BC and the narrow strip of land between the mountains and the Black Sea. According to the sources, the Tauri were the first inhabitants of the Crimean peninsula and never abandoned its borders. They gave their name to the peninsula, which was known in ancient times as Taurica, Taurida and Tauris. Taurians intermixed with the Scythians starting from the end of 3rd century BC, and were mentioned as Tauroscythians and Scythotaurians in the works of ancient Greek writers. The Taurians underwent the rule of the Pontic Kingdom in the 2nd century BAs a result of Roman occupations, Taurians were romanized in the first century AD. Later the Taurians were subsumed by the Alans and Goths, and existed till the 4th century.
Legendary founding of Rome by Romulus and Remus, descendants of Aeneas (Trojan prince). Romulus kills Remus, establishes monarchy.
Persian tribes migrate to Iran from Asia.

column4: Northern Kingdom of Israel to Central Asia
column5: The Assyrian conquest of the Northern Kingdom of Israel leads to the exile of the Ten Lost Tribes, who allegedly migrate eastward through Central Asia. These tribes may have intermingled with Scythian tribes, preserving their Israelite traditions and bloodlines. The Scythians, known for their vast trade networks, could have facilitated the spread of Israelite cultural elements to East Asia.
The Saka-Scythians established extensive trade networks across Eurasia, with some groups reaching the Korean Peninsula.
Persians settle in Persis under King Teispes.
Reign of Lucius Tarquinius Priscus, Etruscan king who expands Rome's infrastructure.
Zoroaster's teachings spread in Persia. (Status: needs confirmation)
Birth of Cyrus II (later known as Cyrus the Great).

In ancient times Gandhara was a trade crossroads and cultural meeting place between India, Central Asia, and the Middle East. The region was subject to Achaemenian Persia in the 6th and 5th centuries BCE and was conquered by Alexander the Great in the 4th century BCE. It was thereafter ruled by the Mauryan dynasty of India, under whom it became a centre for the spread of Buddhism to Afghanistan and Central Asia. Gandhara was then successively ruled by Indo-Greeks, Shakas, Parthians, and Kushans. After its conquest by Maḥmūd of Ghazna in the 11th century CE, the region was held by various Muslim dynasties. Taxila and Peshawar, ancient Gandhara's chief cities, were important cultural centres. From the 1st century BCE to the 6th–7th century CE, Gandhara was the home of a distinctive art style that was a mixture of Indian Buddhist and Greco-Roman influences.
Destruction of Solomon's Temple in Jerusalem.
Eclipse predicted by Thales of Miletus.
Pythagoras born in Samos.

Estimated birth date of Siddhartha Gautama (Buddha) according to some scholars. Some estimates between 563–483 BCE place Siddhartha Gautama's life, where he renounces his royal birthright to seek enlightenment, leading a spiritual revolution reestablishing a lost dharmic kingdom.
Death of king Cyrus I of Anshan.
Cyrus II becomes king of Anshan.
Power struggles in multiple kingdoms (Persia, Babylon, Egypt, Israel).
Nabonidus becomes the last king of the Neo-Babylonian Empire.
Cyrus revolts against the Median Empire.
Cyrus defeats Astyages, king of Medes, and captures Ecbatana, establishing the Achaemenid Empire.
The Persian Empire dominates the Near East, facilitating cultural exchanges between Mesopotamia, Central Asia, and the steppe.
Cyrus conquers Lydia, defeating King Croesus and capturing Sardis.
Persia occupies Cyprus.
Traditional date of Buddha's enlightenment according to some scholar's estimates.
Cyrus conquers Babylon.
Edict of Cyrus allows Jews to return to Jerusalem.
Description

Reign of Lucius Tarquinius Superbus ("Tarquin the Proud"), last Roman king. Acquires the Sibylline Books—prophetic texts consulted during crises—after the Cumaean Sibyl offers nine scrolls; he buys the final three after she burns six.
Cyrus dies in battle against the Massagetae; buried at Pasargadae. Cambyses II succeeds his father Cyrus.
Cambyses II becomes King of Persia.
Cambyses II conquers Egypt, Nubia, and Cyrenaica.

The Lost Army of Cambyses - a historical mystery dating back to when Cambyses II allegedly sent 50,000 soldiers to destroy the Oracle of Amun at Siwa Oasis in Egypt. According to Herodotus, this army vanished in the Western Desert, supposedly engulfed by a massive sandstorm. Alternative theories have emerged, including the possibility of an ambush by Egyptian rebels. Despite numerous expeditions and claims of discoveries, no conclusive evidence of the army's fate has been found.
Cambyses dies under mysterious circumstances while returning to Persia. Some stories suggest he had died from self-inflicted wound to the leg while trying to mount his horse.

The Behistun Inscription states that Gaumata had falsely claimed to be Bardiya, who had actually been killed by Cambyses II. According to Darius, the Iranian people had grown rebellious against Cambyses's rule, and on March 11, 522 BCE, a revolt broke out in his absence. On July 1, the people chose to follow Gaumata, believing him to be Bardiya.
Gaumata Smerdis–Baridya assumes the throne and reigns for 7 months.

Darius I seizes the Persian throne. Darius I and six other nobles assassinate Gaumata. The six nobles who assisted Darius in this coup were: 1. Otanes, 2. Intaphrenes, 3. Gobryas, 4. Hydarnes, 5. Megabyzus, 6. Aspathines. Darius believed that Ahura Mazda had appointed him to rule the Achaemenid Empire.
Darius I becomes King of Persia.
Revolts against Darius begin in Persis, Media, Parthia, Assyria, and Egypt. Precipitating the Achaemenid Civil War (522-520 BC).
Darius fights the Scythians.

The Royal Road is rebuilt for trade by Darius the Great. Darius I built the road to facilitate rapid communication on the western part of his large empire from Susa to Sardis. Mounted couriers of the Angarium were supposed to travel 1,677 miles (2,699 km) from Susa to Sardis in nine days; the journey took ninety days on foot. Herodotus wrote, "There is nothing in the world that travels faster than these Persian couriers." Herodotus's praise for these messengers— "Neither snow nor rain nor heat nor gloom of night stays these couriers from the swift completion of their appointed rounds"— was inscribed on the James Farley Post Office in New York and is sometimes thought of as the United States Postal Service creed.
Construction of the Second Temple in Jerusalem begins by Darius I.
Darius campaigns in India, against Scythians, and in Macedonia and Thrace.
Darius begins construction of Persepolis.
Darius I invades Scythia.
Darius moves the capital from Pasargadae to Persepolis.
Completion of the Second Temple in Jerusalem.
Darius I of Persia campaigns against the Scythians, forcing some tribes to migrate eastward.
Apadana coin hoard deposited (underneath gold foundation tablets of Darius I for the Apadana Palace).
Description

Reign of Lucius Tarquinius Superbus ("Tarquin the Proud"), last Roman king. Acquires the Sibylline Books—prophetic texts consulted during crises—after the Cumaean Sibyl offers nine scrolls; he buys the final three after she burns six.

The rape of Lucretia by Sextus Tarquinius (son of Tarquinius Superbus) sparks aristocratic revolt. Lucius Junius Brutus and Lucius Tarquinius Collatinus lead uprising, exile Tarquin family. Roman Republic established with two consuls; Brutus executes his sons for plotting monarchy's return.
Etruscan king Lars Porsenna besieges Rome to restore Tarquin rule. Republic survives, consolidating power.
Beginning of democratic government in Athens following the expulsion of tyrants.
Confucius begins his teaching career.

Book of Daniel originated as a collection of stories among the Jewish community in Babylon and Mesopotamia, later expanded by visions in chapters 7-12 during the Maccabean era. Daniel possibly chosen as hero due to reputation as a wise seer in Hebrew tradition.
Ionian Greek cities revolt against Persian rule.
Mahavira, founder of Jainism, attains enlightenment.

Dorians from Heraclea Pontica found the sea port of Chersonesos in southwestern Crimea. Initially a democracy ruled by elected archons and a council called the Demiurgi, later becoming more oligarchiEventually expanded northwest, built fortifications, and later submitted to the Bosporan Kingdom around 110 BCE.
Republic defeats Tarquin's son-in-law, Octavius Mamilius, ending royalist revival hopes.
Persian forces begin their first invasion of mainland Greece.
Greeks defeat Persian forces in a decisive battle.
Death of Persian king Darius I; succeeded by his son Xerxes I.
Legendary general Coriolanus exiled, later leads Volsci against Rome before relenting.
Description

Reign of Lucius Tarquinius Superbus ("Tarquin the Proud"), last Roman king. Acquires the Sibylline Books—prophetic texts consulted during crises—after the Cumaean Sibyl offers nine scrolls; he buys the final three after she burns six.

The rape of Lucretia by Sextus Tarquinius (son of Tarquinius Superbus) sparks aristocratic revolt. Lucius Junius Brutus and Lucius Tarquinius Collatinus lead uprising, exile Tarquin family. Roman Republic established with two consuls; Brutus executes his sons for plotting monarchy's return.
Etruscan king Lars Porsenna besieges Rome to restore Tarquin rule. Republic survives, consolidating power.
Beginning of democratic government in Athens following the expulsion of tyrants.
Confucius begins his teaching career.

Book of Daniel originated as a collection of stories among the Jewish community in Babylon and Mesopotamia, later expanded by visions in chapters 7-12 during the Maccabean era. Daniel possibly chosen as hero due to reputation as a wise seer in Hebrew tradition.
Ionian Greek cities revolt against Persian rule.
Mahavira, founder of Jainism, attains enlightenment.

Dorians from Heraclea Pontica found the sea port of Chersonesos in southwestern Crimea. Initially a democracy ruled by elected archons and a council called the Demiurgi, later becoming more oligarchiEventually expanded northwest, built fortifications, and later submitted to the Bosporan Kingdom around 110 BCE.
Republic defeats Tarquin's son-in-law, Octavius Mamilius, ending royalist revival hopes.
Persian forces begin their first invasion of mainland Greece.
Greeks defeat Persian forces in a decisive battle.
Death of Persian king Darius I; succeeded by his son Xerxes I.
Legendary general Coriolanus exiled, later leads Volsci against Rome before relenting.
Xerxes puts down rebellion in Egypt.
Xerxes crushes rebellion in Babylonia.
Xerxes' army invades Greece; Battles of Thermopylae and Salamis.
Athens sacked and burned; Persians defeated at Plataea and Mycale.
Delian League defeats Persians.
Xerxes and his heir Darius assassinated; Artaxerxes I becomes king.
Twelve Tables codify Roman law, easing patrician-plebeian tensions.
Egyptian rebellion crushed despite Athenian support.
War between Athens and Sparta and their respective allies.
Death of Artaxerxes I; succeeded by Darius II.
Athens loses war; Darius II succeeded by Artaxerxes II.
Cyrus the Younger fails to overthrow Artaxerxes II.

Karna (also known as Vasusena, Anga-Raja, Sutaputra and Radheya), a major character in the Hindu epic Mahābhārata. The story parallels the life of Gautama and Darius, connecting this period and civilizational transition.
Gauls sack Rome; Camillus rescues city (Sibylline Books possibly consulted for recovery rituals).
King's Peace returns Ionia to Persian control.

Aristotle (384-322 BCE) mentions a small, deadly serpent in his work Historia Animalium. Later named "basilisk" (meaning "little king") by Pliny the Elder, described as a venomous serpent with a crown-like crest that could kill with its gaze or breath. This account became the foundation for medieval and Renaissance descriptions.
Reconquest of Egypt by the Persian Empire.
Birth of Alexander the Great.
Alexander is tutored by the philosopher Aristotle.
Alexander joins his father Philip II's army.
Alexander leads the cavalry charge at the Battle of Chaeronea.
Philip II is assassinated; Alexander becomes king of Macedonia.
Alexander invades the Persian Empire, crossing into Asia Minor.
Alexander defeats Darius III at the Battle of Issus.
Alexander conquers Egypt and founds Alexandria.
Alexander defeats Darius III decisively and declares himself "King of Asia."
Alexander captures and burns Persepolis. The Achaemenid Empire falls.
Alexander's campaign in India reconnects Greek and Buddhist traditions.
Alexander defeats King Porus.
Alexander dies in Babylon at age 32.
Wars among Alexander's successors for control of his empire.
Seleucus I Nicator establishes the Seleucid Empire.
Description
Gold crowns of Silla (5th-6th century CE) display tree-of-life motifs compared to Assyrian designs. These motifs may reflect a shared cultural heritage with Saka migrations.

Taurians intermixed with the Scythians starting from the end of 3rd century BC, and were mentioned as Tauroscythians and Scythotaurians in the works of ancient Greek writers. The Taurians underwent the rule of the Pontic Kingdom in the 2nd century BAs a result of Roman occupations, Taurians were romanized in the first century AD. Later the Taurians were subsumed by the Alans and Goths, and existed till the 4th century.

Ashoka converts to Buddhism and initiates widespread missionary efforts. He sends emissaries to regions including Syria, Egypt, Greece, Macedonia, and Cyrene. Bilingual inscriptions in Greek and Aramaic from Kandahar indicate cultural exchange between Greek settlers and Indian thought. Early Christianity and Buddhist interactions: Buddhist gravestones in Alexandria depict Dharma wheels, suggesting the presence of Buddhists in Egypt during Christianity's emergence.

Apollonius of Rhodes was an ancient Greek author, best known for the Argonautica, an epic poem about Jason and the Argonauts and their quest for the Golden Fleece. The poem is one of the few extant examples of the epic genre and it was both innovative and influential, providing Ptolemaic Egypt with a "cultural mnemonic" or national "archive of images", and offering the Latin poets Virgil and Gaius Valerius Flaccus a model for their own epics.

Buddhism flourishes in the eastern Greek world, particularly in Bactria. Greco-Buddhism emerges as a fusion of Greek and Buddhist traditions. King Menander I (Milinda) converts to Buddhism and becomes a major patron of Buddhist philosophy.
After catastrophic Roman loss at Cannae (Second Punic War), Sibylline Books advise human sacrifices and importing Cybele's cult.

The cult of Bacchus was introduced to Rome around 200 BCE, and the mystery cult attracted both men and women participants alike. The cult held secret meetings known as the Bacchanalia, festivals dedicated in honour of Bacchus (the Greco-Roman god of wine, religious ecstasy, insanity, and festivity, among other things). According to Livy, the Bacchanalia would have been the perfect place to hatch a plot and/or commit a crime, as any noise made would be drowned out by ceremonial drums and cymbals.
In one of the earliest cases of the Romans coming down hard on a religion, the state violently suppressed the worship of Bacchus in Italy.
The Indo-Greek Kingdom continues as a center for Greco-Buddhist culture. Prominent Buddhist missionaries like Mahadharmaraksita spread Buddhist teachings.

Menander I Soter (lit. 'Menander the Saviour'; Pali: Milinda), sometimes called Menander the Great, was a Greco-Bactrian and later Indo-Greek King (reigned 165/155–130 BC) who administered a large territory in the Northwestern regions of the Indian Subcontinent and Central Asia. Menander is noted for having become a patron and convert to Greco-Buddhism and he is widely regarded as the greatest of the Indo-Greek kings. The Milinda Panha records his dialogues with Buddhist monks.
Description
Sibylline prophecy—"only a king can defeat Parthia"—fuels senatorial fears of Julius Caesar's ambition, contributing to his assassination.
Original Sibylline Books destroyed in Temple of Jupiter fire; replacements compiled under Augustus.
Ebionites emerge as a Jewish-Christian sect. They reject Paul's teachings, adhere to Jewish law, and view Jesus as a human prophet rather than divine.
Gnosticism begins to develop. Gnostics emphasize secret knowledge (gnosis) for salvation and often depict the material world as evil, created by a lesser deity (Demiurge).
Legio IX Hispana (Ninth Legion) vanishes from records. Theories suggest annihilation in northern Britain or transfer east.
Marcionism arises, founded by Marcion of Sinope. Marcion rejects the Old Testament and claims the God of Jesus is distinct from the God of the Hebrew Bible.
Montanism, a charismatic movement led by Montanus, emphasizes prophecy, asceticism, and the imminent return of Christ. It is criticized for its ecstatic practices and claims of new revelation.
Sabellianism (Modalism) emerges, teaching that the Father, Son, and Holy Spirit are not distinct persons but different modes of the same God. It is condemned as heretical for denying the Trinity.

Novatianism arises after the Decian persecution. Novatianists argue that those who apostatized under persecution cannot be readmitted to the Church, opposing the mainstream practice of reconciliation.
Manichaeism, founded by Mani, blends Christian, Gnostic, and Zoroastrian elements. It teaches a dualistic cosmology of light vs. darkness and is later condemned as heretical.

Keyhole tombs (3rd-6th century CE) in Japan are compared to Hyksos-Era burial mounds in the Caucasus. This architectural similarity may indicate a shared cultural heritage between the Hyksos, Scythians, and Japanese.

Arianism, founded by Arius, teaches that Jesus is a created being and not co-eternal with the Father. This sparks the Arian Controversy, leading to the Council of Nicaea (325 CE), which condemns Arianism and affirms the Nicene Creed.
Description
Gold crowns of Silla (5th-6th century CE) display tree-of-life motifs compared to Assyrian designs. These motifs may reflect a shared cultural heritage with Saka migrations.

Ashoka converts to Buddhism and initiates widespread missionary efforts. He sends emissaries to regions including Syria, Egypt, Greece, Macedonia, and Cyrene. Bilingual inscriptions in Greek and Aramaic from Kandahar indicate cultural exchange between Greek settlers and Indian thought. Early Christianity and Buddhist interactions: Buddhist gravestones in Alexandria depict Dharma wheels, suggesting the presence of Buddhists in Egypt during Christianity's emergence.

Taurians intermixed with the Scythians starting from the end of 3rd century BCE, and were mentioned as Tauroscythians and Scythotaurians in the works of ancient Greek writers. The Taurians underwent the rule of the Pontic Kingdom in the 2nd century BCE. As a result of Roman occupations, Taurians were romanized in the first century AD. Later the Taurians were subsumed by the Alans and Goths, and existed till the 4th century.
Condemns Arianism and affirms the Nicene Creed.
Donatism argues that the validity of sacraments depends on the moral purity of the clergy. It is condemned as schismatic.
Priscillianism, an ascetic and Gnostic-influenced movement, emerges in Spain. Priscillian, its leader, is executed for heresy, marking the first instance of capital punishment for heresy.

Theophilus discovers a hidden pagan temple and displays artifacts mockingly, provoking pagan attacks. Christians counter-attack, forcing pagans to retreat to the Serapeum. Emperor orders pardon for pagans but destruction of the temple. Theophilus destroys the Serapeum and Mithraeum, with pagan symbols paraded through the forum. The event symbolizes Christianity's triumph over other religions.

column2: Epiphanius writes to John of Jerusalem
column5: Epiphanius asks for Origen to be condemned.
Jerome, initially an admirer of Origen, aligns with the anti-Origenist faction, accusing Origen's teachings of promoting heresy (e.g., the pre-existence of souls and universal salvation).
Rufinus publishes a Latin translation of Origen's "On First Principles".

Neoplatonism, particularly through thinkers like Plotinus and Porphyry, influences Christian theology. Church Fathers such as Augustine integrate Neoplatonic ideas about the soul, God, and the nature of reality into Christian doctrine.
Theophilus of Alexandria condemns Origen's teachings, leading to the expulsion of Origenist monks from Egyptian monasteries. This marks the height of the First Origenist Crisis.
Description

Theophilus issues a Festal Letter (pastoral letter) denouncing anthropomorphic views of God. A large mob of Alexandrian monks who held anthropomorphic views riots in the streets. Theophilus suddenly changes his position and begins denouncing Origen to appease the monks. The letter affirmed God's incorporeality, consistent with Origenist teaching. Three of four monastic abbots in Nitria refuse to allow it to be read in their communities. Angry desert ascetics travel to Alexandria to demonstrate against Theophilus, threatening violence. This marks the beginning of Theophilus' campaign against Origenism, despite his previous support for Origen's followers.
Theophilus summons a council in Alexandria, which condemns Origen and his followers as heretics.
Theophilus expels Origenist monks from Egyptian monasteries and banishes the four "Tall Brothers" who were leaders of the Nitrian community.

John Chrysostom, Patriarch of Constantinople, grants asylum to the expelled Tall Brothers. Theophilus uses this fact to orchestrate John Chrysostom's condemnation and removal from his position at the Synod of the Oak.
Theophilus restores normal relations with the Origenist monks in Egypt, marking the end of the first Origenist crisis.
Council condemns Pelagianism, which emphasizes human free will and denies original sin.
Council condemns Nestorianism, which teaches that Christ exists as two distinct persons (divine and human) rather than one united person. The council also condemns Pelagianism.
Council condemns Monophysitism, associated with Eutyches, which teaches that Christ has only one nature (divine) rather than two (divine and human).

Execution of Boethius, who had laid groundwork for integrating classical philosophy into Christian theology. This event, shortly before the Academy's closure, accelerated the shift from pagan to Christian philosophy. In the Byzantine Empire, Christian Neoplatonism became the dominant intellectual framework, with figures like Pseudo-Dionysius the Areopagite blending Platonic ideas with Christian mysticism.
Description

Justinian I ordered the closing of the Platonic Academy in Athens. The Academy's property was confiscated, and its funding ceased. This was part of Justinian's broader campaign to suppress paganism and consolidate Christian orthodoxy across the Byzantine Empire. Neoplatonist philosophers flee to the Sasanian Empire, influencing later Islamic philosophy.
Damascius (the last head of the Academy) and Simplicius of Cilicia, along with other Neoplatonists, flee the Byzantine Empire to avoid persecution.

The philosophers arrive in the Sasanian Empire, ruled by Khosrow I (Anushirwan), who was known for his interest in Greek philosophy and science. Khosrow I welcomes the philosophers and provides them with patronage, allowing them to continue their intellectual work.

Khosrow I establishes a scholarly environment in his court, where Greek, Persian, and Indian intellectual traditions are studied and translated. Damascius and Simplicius engage in writing commentaries on classical texts, particularly the works of Plato and Aristotle. Simplicius writes extensive commentaries on Aristotle's works, including the Physics, On the Heavens, and Categories, which later become influential in both Islamic and Byzantine philosophy.

column2: Translation and Preservation of Greek Texts
column5: The Sasanian Empire becomes a hub for the translation of Greek texts into Middle Persian (Pahlavi). These translations later serve as a bridge for the transmission of Greek knowledge to the Islamic world. The philosophers may have collaborated with Persian scholars to translate and preserve Greek philosophical and scientific works.

The Sasanian court under Khosrow I is a melting pot of ideas, where Greek philosophy interacts with Zoroastrian, Indian, and Syriac Christian thought. This cross-cultural exchange enriches the intellectual landscape and lays the groundwork for later developments in Islamic philosophy.

With the death of Khosrow I, the patronage of Greek philosophers in the Sasanian Empire declines. His successors are less interested in intellectual pursuits and more focused on military and political challenges. The philosophers lose their primary source of support and begin to disperse.

Some philosophers, including Simplicius, may have returned to the Byzantine Empire, where they continued their work in relative obscurity. Others may have remained in the Sasanian Empire or moved to other centers of learning in the Eastern Mediterranean.

The Sasanian Empire is weakened by a prolonged war with the Byzantine Empire (602-628) and later falls to the Islamic Caliphate during the Arab-Muslim conquests (633-651). The decline of the Sasanian Empire leads to the dispersal of its intellectual community, including scholars who had preserved Greek texts.

The Greek texts preserved and translated in the Sasanian Empire become a key resource for the Islamic Golden Age. The House of Wisdom in Baghdad, established by the Abbasid Caliphs in the 8th-9th centuries, becomes a center for the translation of Greek works into ArabiPhilosophers like Al-Kindi, Al-Farabi, and Avicenna (Ibn Sina) engage with these texts, blending Greek philosophy with Islamic theology.
Description

Philosophers like Damascius and Simplicius continued their work under Khosrow I's patronage. They likely contributed to the translation and preservation of Greek texts, which later influenced Islamic philosophy. After Khosrow's death and the decline of the Sasanian Empire, many of these philosophers may have returned to the Byzantine Empire or dispersed further east.

Alexandria remained a center of learning, though it increasingly focused on Christian theology and Aristotelian logiFigures like John Philoponus and Olympiodorus the Younger continued to teach and write, blending classical philosophy with Christian thought. The School of Alexandria became a key institution for the study of Aristotle and Neoplatonism within a Christian context.

Greek philosophical texts, including works by Plato, Aristotle, and the Neoplatonists, were translated into Arabic during the Islamic Golden Age. This was facilitated by the House of Wisdom in Baghdad and other centers of learning. Philosophers like Al-Kindi, Al-Farabi, and Avicenna (Ibn Sina) engaged deeply with Greek thought, adapting it to Islamic theology and philosophy.

Neoplatonic ideas, particularly those of Plotinus and Proclus, were integrated into Islamic metaphysics and mysticism (e.g., Sufism). The works of the Athenian philosophers who fled to the Sasanian Empire may have indirectly influenced this intellectual tradition.
In Western Europe, the works of classical philosophers were preserved and transmitted through monastic scriptoria. The Carolingian Renaissance saw renewed interest in classical thought.

The Byzantine Empire saw a revival of classical learning during the Macedonian Renaissance. Figures like Michael Psellos and John Italus engaged with Neoplatonism and Aristotelianism, though within a Christian framework.

Hidden communities in Japan, such as the Eta caste, are theorized to have preserved Levantine taboos on leatherworking. Evidence: Zenko-ji Temple's hexagonal floor patterns and "Ark of the Covenant" analogies suggest possible Occidental influences.
Renewed interest in classical thought during the 12th-century Renaissance.
Thinkers like Thomas Aquinas synthesized Aristotelian philosophy with Christian theology, shaping medieval scholasticism.
The Battle of the Kalka River marks the first major encounter between the Kievan Rus' and the Mongol Empire.
Description

The Hyksos controlled northern Egypt before being expelled and migrating northward. They may have carried Mesopotamian religious symbols into Central Asia, influencing later Scythian and Saka cultures.
The Assyrian conquest of the Northern Kingdom of Israel in 722 BCE led to the exile of the Ten Lost Tribes, who allegedly migrated eastward through Central Asia.
The Saka-Scythians established extensive trade networks across Eurasia, with some groups reaching the Korean Peninsula.
Aristotle mentioned a small, deadly serpent in his work Historia Animalium, though he did not explicitly call it a "basilisk."
The Silla Kingdom is founded by Bak Hyeokgeose, described with "radiant light" motifs compared to menorah symbolism.
An embassy from Southern India visits Caesar Augustus in Samos. The sage Zarmanochegas performs self-immolation in Athens. His tomb identifies him as a Shramana (Buddhist ascetic).
Pliny the Elder in his Natural History described the basilisk as a venomous serpent with a crown-like crest, capable of killing with its gaze or breath.
Buddhist gravestones from the Ptolemaic period found in Alexandria depict Dharma wheels, suggesting Buddhist presence during Christianity's emergence.

Description

Pharnavaz becomes the first king after emerging victorious in a power struggle. He built Armaztsikhe citadel, a temple to Armazi, and created a new administrative system dividing the country into counties (saeristavos).
End of Pharnavaz I's reign, beginning a period of prosperity followed by incessant warfare against numerous invasions.
Southern parts of Iberia, previously conquered from Armenia, are reunited with Armenia. Colchian lands secede to form separate princedoms (sceptuchoi).
Pharnavazid king Pharnajom is dethroned by his subjects after converting to Zoroastrianism.
Armenian prince Artaxias ascends the Iberian throne, establishing the Artaxiad dynasty of Iberia.
Roman general Pompey invades Iberia during his war with Mithradates VI of Pontus and Armenia. Rome does not establish permanent power over Iberia.
Romans march on Iberia again, forcing King Pharnavaz II to join their campaign against Caucasian Albania.
Iberia accepts Roman Imperial protection while maintaining independence, becoming a free ally rather than a province.
King described in stone inscription at Mtskheta as "the friend of the Caesars" and king "of the Roman-loving Iberians."
Emperor Vespasian fortifies the ancient Mtskheta site of Arzami for the Iberian kings.
Iberia regains some of its former power. Relations with Emperor Hadrian were initially strained but improved under Antoninus Pius.
Rome recognizes Iberia as an ally rather than a subject state, a situation that remained even during hostilities with the Parthians.
Foundation of the Sasanian Empire, which changes Iberia's political orientation away from Rome.
During the reign of Sasanian ruler Shapur I, Iberia becomes a tributary of the Persian state.
The Iberian king is listed as a high dignitary of the Sasanian realm and cooperates in Persian campaigns against Rome.
Probable establishment of Zoroastrianism in Iberia as Sasanians propagate their religion.
The treaty grants the Roman Empire control of Iberia again as a vassal state. Rome recognizes Mirian III as king, the first of the Chosroid dynasty.
Saint Nino begins preaching Christianity in Iberia.
King Mirian III and leading nobles convert to Christianity, declaring it the state religion, creating strong ties with Rome/Byzantium.
After Emperor Julian's death during his failed Persian campaign, Rome cedes control of Iberia to Persia. King Varaz-Bakur I becomes a Persian vassal.
The treaty confirms Persia's control over Iberia.
King Pharsman IV temporarily preserves his country's autonomy and ceases to pay tribute to Persia.
Zoroastrianism becomes a second official religion alongside Christianity under Sasanian influence.
Period marked by relative revival. Though a Persian vassal, he secures northern borders, subjugates mountaineers, and brings western and southern Georgian lands under control.
Vakhtang leads a general uprising against Persia, starting a twenty-year war for independence that ultimately fails.
Vakhtang I dies in battle against Persia.
Another unsuccessful insurrection against Persia has severe consequences. Iberian kings hold only nominal power while Persians effectively rule.
Following King Bacurius III's death, Sasanian ruler Hormizd IV abolishes the Iberian monarchy. Iberia becomes a Persian province ruled by a marzpan (governor).
Georgian nobles urge Byzantine emperor Maurice to revive the kingdom of Iberia.
Byzantium and Persia agree to divide Iberia, with Tbilisi in Persian hands and Mtskheta under Byzantine control.
Iberian prince Stephan I joins forces with Persia to reunite all territories of Iberia.
Heraclius's offensive brings victory over Georgians and Persians, ensuring Byzantine predominance until the Arab invasion.
Arabs reach Iberia and force Prince Stephanoz II to abandon Byzantine allegiance and recognize the Caliph. Iberia becomes a tributary state.
An Arab emir is installed in Tbilisi.
Ashot I establishes himself as hereditary prince of Iberia with the Byzantine title kouropalates during weakening Arab rule.
Adarnase IV, formally a Byzantine vassal, is crowned as "king of Iberia."
Bagrat III brings various principalities together to form a united Georgian monarchy, marking the transition from the Kingdom of Iberia to the Kingdom of Georgia.

column2: Establishment of the Republic of Turkey
column5: The Republic of Turkey is established under Mustafa Kemal Atatürk's leadership5.

column2: Formation of Turkic and Mongol confederations
column5: The Turkic and Mongol tribes, ancestors of the Tatars, begin to form nomadic confederations in Central Asia and the steppes of Eurasia.

column2: Kipchak Turks dominate the Pontic-Caspian steppe
column5: The Kipchak Turks interact with Slavic tribes and the Kievan Rus'.
The first major encounter between the Kievan Rus' and the Mongol Empire, resulting in a Mongol victory.
The Mongols destroy major cities like Kiev, establishing the Golden Horde.
The Golden Horde is established as a Mongol successor state. The Tatars become the dominant group within the Horde.

column2: The Golden Horde imposes the "Tatar Yoke" on the Russian principalities
column5: The Golden Horde demands tribute and loyalty from the Russian principalities. Some Russian princes cooperate to maintain their positions.

column3: Dmitry Donskoy of Moscow, Golden Horde
column5: Dmitry Donskoy of Moscow defeats the Golden Horde, marking the beginning of Russian resistance to Tatar rule.

column2: Timur (Tamerlane) invades and devastates the Golden Horde
column5: Timur's invasion weakens the Golden Horde, leading to its fragmentation.
The Golden Horde fragments into smaller khanates, including the Kazan Khanate, Crimean Khanate, and Astrakhan Khanate.

column2: Great Stand on the Ugra River
column3: Ivan III of Moscow, Golden Horde
column5: Ivan III refuses to pay tribute to the Golden Horde, effectively ending the "Tatar Yoke" over Russia and marking Russian independence.
Ivan IV (the Terrible) conquers the Kazan Khanate, incorporating it into the Russian Empire and expanding Russian influence in the Volga region.
Russia conquers the Astrakhan Khanate, further consolidating control over the Volga River region.

column2: Crimean Khanate becomes an Ottoman vassal
column5: The Crimean Khanate gains protection and support from the Ottoman Empire as a vassal state.

column2: Sack of Moscow by Crimean Tatars
column5: Devlet I Giray leads a raid that sacks Moscow, showcasing the continued military power of the Crimean Khanate.
Frequent raids result in plundering resources and capturing slaves from neighboring regions.

column2: Failed Russian campaigns against the Crimean Khanate
column5: Russia's attempts to subdue the Crimean Khanate fail, reflecting broader struggles against Ottoman influence in the region.
Russia challenges Ottoman and Crimean Tatar dominance in the Black Sea region during this conflict.
Ends Ottoman suzerainty over the Crimean Khanate; Crimea becomes a Russian protectorate.
Catherine formally annexes Crimea, ending Tatar independence; many Tatars flee to Ottoman territories.

column2: Russian expansion into Caucasus and Central Asia
column5: Russia incorporates Tatar and other Turkic peoples into its empire through territorial expansion.
The Russian Empire consolidates control over Tatar regions while enforcing Russification policies; Tatar culture and Islam persist.
Advocates for modern education and cultural reform among Muslim Tatars under Russian rule.
Russia expands influence in these regions while weakening Ottoman control.
Collapse of the Russian Empire leads to attempts by Tatars to establish independent states in Kazan and Crimea.
The Tatar Autonomous Soviet Socialist Republic is created within the Soviet Union with limited autonomy for Volga Tatars.
Stalin accuses Crimean Tatars of Nazi collaboration during WWII and forcibly deports them to Central Asia, nearly eradicating their presence in Crimea.

column2: Repression of Tatars under Soviet rule
column5: Despite repression, Tatar culture and identity persist in diaspora communities across Soviet territories.
Collapse of the Soviet Union allows for cultural and religious revival; Republic of Tatarstan gains significant autonomy within Russia.
Russia's annexation leads to tensions with Crimean Tatars who largely oppose Russian rule.
Tatars maintain their language, culture, and Islamic traditions while playing a significant role in modern Russian society.
The Hata Clan settles in Japan, introducing silk production and other cultural practices.
The Göktürk Khaganate emerges, influencing Turkic and Mongolic tribes, including the Tatars.
The Tatar Confederation becomes one of the five major tribal confederations on the Mongolian Plateau.
The Uyghur Khaganate dominates Central Asia, influencing the Tatars and other nomadic groups.
The Toquz Tatars ally with the Sekiz-Oghuz in a failed revolt against the Uyghur Khaganate.
The Abbasid Caliphate rises, facilitating trade and cultural exchanges across Eurasia.
Genghis Khan conquers the Tatar Confederation, absorbing its people into the Mongol Empire.
The Mongol Empire becomes the largest contiguous empire in history, connecting East Asia, Central Asia, and Europe.
The Kamakura Shogunate establishes a feudal system in Japan, influenced by continental migrations.
Japan shows interest in Jewish refugees during WWII, known as the Fugu Plan.
The Holocaust leads to the displacement and genocide of Jewish populations in Europe.
Advances in genetic research reveal connections between Eurasian populations, including Turkic, Mongolic, and East Asian groups.
